At the forum the workers who
are not members of labor organiza
tions are given courteous opportun
ity to discuss the union question and
to state their reasons for holding
aloof from the labor movement. It is
too early as yet to gauge the success
of these meetings from the stand
point of increasing the union member
ship, but there is no question of the
interest they have aroused among
workers who might have given little
thought to joining a trade union. The
meetings are addressed by officers of
local unions or delegates to the cen
tral bodies, the form being plain and
simple talks' on the value and neces
sity of the union in the daily life of
the worker.
Other central bodies have laid
plans for naming large organization
committees to work in relays, so that
no member of a committee will be
called upon to speak more than one
night in a week. These .committees
are now composed of those officers
of local unions and delegates to cen
tral bodies who volunteer to serve.
They devote their chief efforts to
arousing general interest in the work
of organized labor among trade union
ists by addressing regular meetings
of local unions, usually giving a talk
lasting not more than ten minutes
It is believed that this plan will prove
to be so popular that it will serve
to stimulate such activity that volun­

These meetings are held at noon

teers will come forward in numbers
sufficient to meet every demand made
upon the unions for speakers to dis
cuss any phase of the labor question.
This work that has been started
will result in a widespread campaign
of education in the labor movement
that will develop a spirit of unity
among the workers that will be proof
against any attacks the enemies of
labor may make.
"A MEASURE OF
PROSPERITY"
Perhaps it is more forcibly impress
ed on us at the present time that the
wages paid weekly to a community's
workers is the true measure of any
community's prosperity. Machinery
can add much wealth to a few indi
viduals, but it fails to spread over
community.
The girls of the Beckett Paper Com
pany have shown the right spirit in
the unemployment situation. An
offer was made the girls to divide and
Ane-half work a week, then the other
half work a week. The girls said no
all of us will work a part of each
week, and we get a small pay each
week. An adaptation of this method
will relieve the unemployment situ
ation, as it will spread the idleness
more evenly, and prevent any neces
sity of bread lines. It is a method that
will be acceptable to the workers.
At one time in Covington, Ky.,
there lived a construction contractor
who made it a practice each fall when
work began to slacken to divide his
crew of men into two batches. His
week ended on Wednesday. The first
week he would work number one
batch the next week number two.
Each week all of his men had a pay
day. And he had the pick of men to
work for him, he knew'where to find
men anytime he needed them on a
hurry up job, and all of his workers
were better able to carry themselves
through slack seasons. This is a gold
en rule idea carried into actual busi
ness in the end business is the gainer.
Men always reciprocate by giving
more service, and all carry a portion
of the slackened employment's cost.

Washington.—Plans to arouse the
citizenship of America in support of
the purpose underlying the Interna
tional conference on limitation of ar
mament have been announced by the
American Federation of Labor in let
ters sent to all central labor bodies
by President Samuel Gompers by di
rection of the executive council.
The plans call for a great national
demonstration on Armistice Day, the
day on which the international con
ference meets in Washington to begin
its work of devising methods of de
creasing armament.
Through the national headquarters
of the A. F.- of L. and through the
central labor bodies it is planned to
enlist the co-operation of other organ
izations in order that the Armistice
Day demonstrations may be represen
tative of the entire citizenship, arous
ed to the vital nature of the issue.
The plan for Armistice Day demon
nation revolves around the central
xr bodies in thecities and commun
es throughout the country. Upon
sir effort hinges the success of the
tire movement.
Military Basis ftlust Be Put Aside
The letter sent by President Gom
rs to the centrals follows, in part:
"The executive council of the Amer
in Federation of Labor has decided
at American organized labor must
ke the lead in impressing upon the
ternational conference for the limi
tion of armament the overwhelming
rld determination to stop conduct
s' international affairs on a military
sis.
"The international conference meets
Washington on November 11, 1921,
on invitation of the American gov
nment.
"November 11 is Armistice Day—
e day on which German autocracy
ve up in defeat.
"Three years ago, on Armistice
ty, democracy won an imperishable
•tory.
"May it win another great victory
this third anniversary.
"In accordance with the action of
e executive council every central
oor body in America is urgently call
upon to perform a definite duty in
der that the disarmament confer
ee may not forget its purpose and
order that/ the largest possible dis
11 mament may be achieved.
"On the day of the opening of the
sarmament conference there should
a great national American demon
ration, giving voice to the thought
i determination of America, back
ing up our government in its leader
ip toward disarmament and giving
urage and determination to the
Qierican representatives in the in
rnational conference.
"The American trade union move
ment, always making a clear distinc
tion between disarmament and pacif
ism, has repeatedly declared for dis
armament, because it has faith in de
mocracy.
"The time ia« come now to speak
with the full volume of our voice, join
ing with all other elements in Ameri
can life and citizenship whose faith
is akin to ours and whose love for
peace is as deep,

"TIME'S COME TO DISARM"
LABOR TAKES THE LEAD IN DISARMAMENT LEGION-CENTRAL
BODIES OF NATION TO ORGANIZE GREAT PARADES
AND MASS MEETINGS
President Gompers Asks all Civic Bodies to Join With Labor in Great
Demonstration on Armistice
Great Demonstration on Armistice
Day
"In every city and town in America
let there be a great demonstration on
Armistice Day!
"It is expected that every city cen
tral body will join in the national
demonstration, leaving no missing
link in the great chain across the
country.
"There should be a parade in every
locality—a great parade for disarma
ment. The parade is the first con
sideration. Suggestions that may be
of assistance in the organization of a
parade will be furnished by American
Federation of Labor headquarters.
These suggestions are intended mere
ly to be helpful, but in addition, if the
central thought in all parades can be
similar, the national effectiveness of
the effort will be increased.
"Every parade should end in a mass
meeting. The best obtainable speak
ers should address these great meet
ings. In ample time an address to
The Time Has Come to Disarm,
Because Militarist Autocra
cies Have Perished
"The fact is that disarma
ment now is possible for prac
tical reasons and not for senti
mental reasons. Sentimental
reasons have existed always, but
the world kept on arming. The
practical thing that stood in the
way of yielding to sentiment
was the necessity of protecting
the advance of democracy
against the constant threat and
menace of autocracy and mili
tarism.
"While the two systems of hu
man organization and govern
ment stood facing each other on
relatively even terms of strength
the cause of disarmament was a
hopeless one.
"The great militarist autoc
racies have perished. A funda
mental change has come over
the peoples formerly ruled by
emperors, kaisers, czars and
conquerers. Self-government
has been gained for practically
the whole civilized world. That
fact, more than any other,
makes disarmament possible to
day."—President Gompers.
the international disarmament confer
ence will be forwarded to you
adoption at the mass meeting, I ri
day, November 11, 1921, Armist v
Day. Adoption of this address every
where will mean the expression of th
determination of millions of Amen
cans in behalf of disarmament.
"It is highly desirable that thi
great Armistice Day disarmament
monstration be not alone the express
ion of labor's views but that it be tin
expression of the views of our citi
zenship, under labor's leadership. La
bor takes the lead because it has
highly developed sense of duty, is de
voutly American, thoroughly demo
cratic, and has the great organiza
tion necessary to leadership in a cru
cial? moment such as this.

"Time Has Come to Disarm!"
"Action is the need of the hour.
"'The time has come to disarm!' is
the slogan.
"The hour has struck to make good
our faith in democracy, to bring suc
cess to a great constructive move
ment in which labor has ever been the
leader.
"Let no man fail, let no woman fail,
let no local union fail, let no central
body fail.
"Let the international disarmament
conference know that the heart of
America beats high in determination.
"Let this be America's greatest de
monstration, America's greatest man
date, America's greatest pledge of
faith for the peace of the world."
In addition to the local community
demonstrations which will weld the
country into a unit in expressing its
opinion to the world conference which
will meet in Washington, President
Gompers has issued invitations to a
number of men and women, including
leaders of thought in all walks of
life, asking them to serve as an ad
visory committee to assist in making
plans that may prove helpful to the
cause of disarmament. It is expect
ed that this committee, which will be
a most notable one, will meet at least
once—and perhaps more than that—
before the international conference
assembles in November.
In all, the plans made by the Amer
ican Federation of Labor are the most
extensive ever made for such an
event and they combine the work of
organizing a great national demon
stration with the work of developing
and applying practical plans that will
prove helpful in achieving the object
for which President Harding has call
ed together representatives of the
great nations of the world.

INCREASE IN COST OF
FOOD
Washington.—From July 15 to Aug
ust 15 there was an increase in the
retail cost of food in 14 principal cit
ies of the United States, according to
the bureau of labor statistics of the
United Staes department of labor. In
Philadelphia food prices advanced 6
!e cent in Bridgeport, Conn., Chi
'.go. New Haven, Conn., Providence,
1., and Washington the rise was
per cent. Kansas City marked up
4 per cent, while in St. Louis, Spring
field, 111., and Birmingham, Ala., the
people gave over the counter 3 per
cent more for their eatables. Peoria,
111., shows a 2 per cent levy on the
family purse. Salt Lake City, Den
ver and Little Rock show an increase
of 1 per cent.

Sailing westward from Nagasaki
one leaves behind the quiet waters
of the Inland Sea and the Pacific and
enters upon the turbid, storm-tossed
waves of the Yellow Sea, the northern
playground of the typhoon.
Shanghai is the nearest great port
of China across this sea. and four days
at most brings one to this first safe
landing on the continent. Almost all
the way there are islands to be seen,
and perhaps the high shores of For
mosa, the unexplored haunt of pirates
and brigands. Far out at sea one
notes that the yellow water has be
come yellower, almost the hue of our
own Missouri River, and soon the
strong current of a great river is en
tered and the low shores of the Yang
tse appear. Vast farmless junks with
battened lugs pitch and pound along
the coast, and are a source of anxiety
at night, as they carry no lights,
either through fear of pirates or for
the sake of economy.
At the mouth of the Yang-tse is
the celebrated "heavenly barrier," the
Woosung Bar. The bay offers little
protection, but we cast anchor here
in company with a huge French mail
steamer and two British tramps and
flew the white flag "U. S. Mail."
There Is no railroad from Woosung
to Shanghai, the one built there hav
ing been destroyed. An hour in a
launch, however, brings one in sight
of this Paris of China and up to the
most imposing Bund in the Orient.
The little gunboats Wilmington and
Villalobos, the latter a Spanish prize,
lay below the American consulate, and
their sailors responded heartily to
our cheers for Old Olory. English
men of war lay near in all the grim
threat of war paint, and small French
and German gunboats idled near the
foreign settlements.
Oddly enough, the first person to
greet one in Shanghai is sure to be a
tall, beturbaned Sikh policeman, who
perhaps speaks neither English nor
Chinese, and carries neither club,
sword nor firearm. Statuesque and
imperturbable, he upholds the ban
ners of British rule and deals out jus
tice to the best of his ability. Euro
pean and American sailors take ad
vantage of his lack of weapons occa
sionally, and send him to the hospital,
but he gets along famously with the
Chinese.

Native Quarter.

Sailors and soldiers are everywhere.
You may see two drunken man-of

coolies into a mad gallop -and betting

sailors, with the heave of the
seas in their gaits, haunt the board
ing-houses along the lower streets,
and English and American regulars
fraternize in the shade.
They say that it always rains in
Hongkong, and one can readily be
lieve it. As a result, the well-paved
streets are clean and the vegetation
on the hills is fresh and green through
out the summer. The native town is
also kept clean and healthy.
The roar of cannon and the shrill
war pipes of a Burma band answer
the ringing cheers of a big English
cruiser which has broken out the long
homeward-bound pennant so dear to
the eye of the sailor. With her go
two torpedo cruisers that are creep
ing northward toward the edge of the
Russian storm cloud. Hongkong be
hind her cannons looks out like the
mandarin from his curtained sedan
chair in the street. What does she
care for these street brawls!
bearers are the steel hulls of the
seven seas, her livery bears the dragon
and St. George. Hef heart is in the
great noisy corridors of the bank.

Weight of Children.
The weight of a growing child is
the most important index to its gen
eral health. A child of five years, for
instance, should weigh about as many
pounds as it is inches high. As a rule
this will be 40 pounds. When a child
is rather heavier in proportion to its
height it is a sign of goocf health. A
deficiency of weight in proportion to
height is always an unfavorable sign.
Any interruption in the progress of
increase of weight, especially during
the continuance of growth, must be a
danger signal that should not be neg
lected.

Dainty women are careful about
their neck fixings. Stocks and col

pattern. The fastidious woman is de
clared by the dainty freshness of her
little turnover collar, the exactness

trig lines of her bow, cravat or tabs.
A slouchily closed stock, a collar that

tray the careless, hit-or-miss person.
It shows a marked lack of sense of
the fitness of things to wear a low
neck bodice in the street and the
string of pearls, such as the New
Yorker so often adds, scarcely betters
the impression. In London such a
style. .)V dressing for the street is con
sidered provincial to a degree.

Nut Sandwiches.
For peanut sandwiches, mix three
tablespoonfuls of mayonnaise dressing
with one cupful of freshly roasted
peanuts chopped very fine and salted
to taste. A more delicious sandwich
is made from the large Brazil or
cream nut. Carefully remove the shell
and drop for a moment into hot water
any of the nut meat having the brown
skin on to remove it quickly. Chop
the nuts very fine, add a suggestion
of red pepper, a bit of lemon juice and
a generous pinch of salt, moisten with
sufficient sweet cream to make a
paste and spread on thin slices of
bread.

put away to come into action.
Sweeping the Carpet.
To clean and brighten a carpet
sprinkle it over with a little dampened
salt before sweeping it. The salt pre
vents the dust from flying, keeps off
moths and brightens the colors

by it is one that,oomes only. after much schooling, and the
vast majority of the rawe does not employ it at all.
Learning a language is making sounds liki those we hear
others make. And this is the very best way.
In any culture one example is worth a dozen precepts.
What, are good manners but doing as 4 our betters.
In any circle it is the sophUstiated who. turn up their
noses at the greenhorns, and all there is to sophistication
is that we have learned to act precisely like all around us.
Civilization itself seems a perfection in apishness, a
state wherein all do, may and think alike.
And what are morals but oonformity .
And good society, courtliness and being a perfect gentle
man or lady means that outwardly at least we have sup
pressed all individuality and each of us -has become "the
mold of fashion and the glass of form."
Speaking of fashion, do not women spend fortunes keep
ing in style, which, when you come to think of it, means
aping their leaders. Consider the billions of dollars spent
by the female sex along Fifth avenue and the Rue de la
Paix in the struggle for simian perfection.
And men also dress not aooording,to any 'intelligent rea
sons whatever, nor by the fitness of things, but solely that
they may be clothed lie other men.
And if anyone will not conform, and appears at the opera
in his shirt sleeves or comes to church without his breeches,
we arrest hin.
We hate the person who will not join in our aping. He
is a crank or a bit cracked.
Even religion, at least orthodoxy, is similaritr of belief.
So deep is our ape inheritance that we must need stone
the wretch who will not think as we all think, and we can
not endure one who id so stubbaft and unreasonable as to
like what we do not like.
Success, what is it? Every successful book, newspaper,
magazine, soap, fountain pen, preacher, actor or what not,
is followed by a host of imitators; besides being "the
thing," or the vogue or all the go and so on, what does *
it mean but that you have started a troop of apes to fancy
you?
And in death, the one place where one ought to be al
lowed to do as one pleases, we are worse off than anywhere
else. For then comes the Funeral, and we are wounded
and shocked when we-see any family that will not conform
to the heathenish, stupid and dreadful custom that there
holds away.
Come from apes? Yes, indeed, and doing our best to go
back.
Why Not Trust Congress? |
HE MORE we consider it, the more unwise appears
the proposal that Congress surrender to the Secre
tary of the Treasury its control over the huge indebt
edness of the Entente Powers to the United States.
We should consider the proposal unwise, no matter
who the Treasury official or what executive officer of the q
Government was intended as the recipient of such uncon
trolled power.
There have been 'many breaches redently in the divi
s ion walls erected by the Constitution to limit the arbitrary
power of our executives. Legislative authority has been
continually seeping out through these breaches into the
executive department.
Congress, has in recent years exercised lees and less
its o*n judgment and discretion; it has more and. more
left important decisions to the Executive. The power of
initiative is almost entirely gone; it retains only a kind
of eruptive, spasmodic and sporadic power of veto.
a-k. This is not the kind of Congress our forefathers con
4. ceived and intended to create. It was not the kind of law
making body which a free country should have.
Our courts, in some cases deliberately, have usurped
some of the President's power and more of the powers of
Congress. These usurpations have to this extent changed
-~ our form of government and deprived our citizens of the
ademocracy guaranteed by the Constitution.
Our forefathers believed that in all matters properly
within the scope of legislative powers the judgment of the
many minds in Congress was safer than the sole judg
ment of an executive.
k. Without doubt the people of Americas today believe
the same thing. They would prefer to trust the judgment
of Congress upon so tremendous a question as the dispo
sition of the ten billion dollars of debt owed this country
m..by foreign nations. They would rather trust the respon
Isiveness of Congress to American public opinion than to
mtrust anybody in teexecutive branch ofour Government,
e no matter how honest or how wise.
Secretary Mellon, an exceedingly keen and astute
ht. financier, should negotiate with our late associate. in war'
a settlement of all their obligations to us, and he should ,
then submit to Congress, for its approval, his proposed
senttlement.

text:
ABERDEEN! HERALD
VOL. XVII
CHINESE LABOR
IN THE PHILIPPINES, OPPOSED
BY LABOR LEADERS.
Recommendations of Prof. Jenks Denounced
in a Public Utter. The JenlM System
Meant the Establishment of Coolie Slaveiy.
Imperialism Disclosing Itself.
Prof. Jenk's report to the secretary of
war on the guesrion of Chinese labor in
the Philippines has attracted the atten
tion and excited the hostility of Snmuel
Gompers, Ernest H. Crosby, Daniel
Harris, A. J. Boulton, Charles B. Spahr
and Henry White, all representative
men, who have issued a public letter de
nouncing Prof. Jenk's recommendations.
They quote the recommendations,
which propose empowering the Philip
pine commission to legislate regarding
the admission of Chinese labor under
contract. The details have evidently
been nicely adjusted with a view to
creating and maintaining a system of
coolie slavery in our Asiatic "posses
sions." Kmployers of "not less than,
say, twenty-five laborers," would bo al
lowed, under Prof. Jenk's system, to
import "Chinese under contract for a
period of not over three years," and the
employers would be required to give
bonds to secure proper treatment of their
imported Chinese coolies, as well as for
their security and employment at~the
work specified in the contract, and their
return to China at its expiration, unless
"the contract is rene.ved under the
special permission of the government
separately in each case."
Over tliis system of coolie slavery an
inspector or "protector of the Chinese,"
appointed by the governor of the Philip
pines, would preside, to rigidly enforce
the law, by among other things, pre
venting the imported coolie from "ab
sconding and going elswhere in the
islands as a regular inhabitant." In
other words, what the delectable Prof,
.lenks calmly proposes is that large em
ployers in the Philippines shall be al
lowed to e tiblsh a system of term serf
dom, the serfs to be bought for importa
tion and re-exported at the end of their
terms, being meanwhile elosely confined
to their masters' domicile. Comment
ing upon this iuiquitious proposal, the
gentlemen named above justly define it
as meaning "the establishment of the
contract system of coolie labor in its
most objectionable form, in many re
spects resembling slavery."
Little by little tlie real purposes of the
policy of imperialism discloses itself to
those workingmen who voteil for a "full
dinner pail" in 1900, with 110 thought or
care for the "obsolete" rights of man
they were hereby tramping upon in the
Philippines. I'rof. Jenk's proposals are
abhorrent, but they are in the regular
order of evolution under the duty and
destiny policy.—The Public.
What's in a Name?
Shakespeare made Juliet say "A rose,
by any other name would smell as sweet."
That these wordß are very true in the
case of the rose, is not doubted, but it's
application (o other things and condi
tions is seriously questioned. For illus
tration : We know that all railrsads
must have scenery, grand, monotonous
or indifferent, but when the "Columbia
River Scenery" on the O. R. & N. is
mentioned, the ttaveler will tell you that
it is different from any he has yet seen.
All highways pass thriving villages,
but, when the interesting and renowned
Utah cities, Ogrien and Salt Lake, on the

DON'T KNOCK.
"Be Up and Doing With a Heart for Any
' Fate."
Take the world as vou find it, young
man, and do the best you can. It may
occur to you that social conditions are
not just right; that in the mad hustle
for food and raiment and the shelter of
a modern roof, some men get too much
and others not enough. It may seem
that homage is paid where it is unde
served. and withheld from these most
entitled to it. All these things which
are wrong merely reflect the imperfec
tions of people in general and everybody
in particular. The world isn't exactly
right socially, politically, morally or
otherwise, anil you can't make it so or
better your own condition by eternally
finding fault with the situation as you
have found it. If you are without
means, you hare no time to waste in
structing other people in their duty to
themselves and to society. An example
of patient industry, prudent economy j
and praiseworthy rectitude of life does ;
more to influence society along the right!
line than all the hot air one pair of lungs !
can generate and give off in a lifetime., j
If any man will carefully study his own i
life, he will discover that he is far short,
of being a model of the perfect man. If,
he is unable to regulate his own conduct
how can he hope to overturn the estab
lished custom of society an<l send the j
world spinning on the road to glory by
merely telling people how thinus ought
to be and what government is in duty
bound to do for the amelioration of ex
isting condition?. Have convictions,
and the courage of the same, but don't
overlook the opportunities to better your
own condition for the mere s.tke of
knocking, when knocking does no good.
However narrow the chances of becom
ing a millionaire, any person in health
has a grand opportunity to earn a living i
and save a little for the time when the
north wind blows. The joy of living is
in the effort to accomplish. Get into the
harness and pull as hard as you can
without breaking a tug. Hold your
head up, look yonr fellow man in the
face, and laugh when you feel like it.
Get busy and stop grumbling.—Nebraska
State Journal.

There was a warm'time at the Chehalis
County Logging Co.'s camp for a little
while last Saturday afternoon. Rock
Stanich, over whose land there was a
short piece of skid toad, made an attempt

be taken. Mr. Stanich is the man who
wanted $1500 damages for right-of-way
for the company's railway,'but a jury
gave him $20.—Vldette.

NEEDED LAWS
LEGISLATION DESIRED BY THE
LUMBER MANUFACTURERS.
Resolution Adopted Asking for LegiiUtior
Requiring Railroads to Supply Cars Im
partially! Creating the Office of Fire
Warden, and Permitting Mutual Fire In
surance.
! At a meeting of the lumber manufac
turers of Washington, held recently, a
■ resolution was presented and adopted
that asks for three pieces of legislation
of particular interest to the lumber
■ manufacturers of this state. The lumber
; interests of Washington constitute the
leading industry of the state. The west
ern part of the state, in particular, has
'sent to tl ; legislature a considerable
! number of lumbermen, and anything
j desired by the lumber industry will have
strong support, especially if it has merit.
; Hence the lumbermen feel that in ask
j ing for reasonable legislation they are in
j a fair way to have their desires granted.
The first request embodied in the reso
lution is for a law that will compel the
' railroads of the state to distribute cars
impartially to the various indu°tries,
and to provide a penalty in case the law
is violated. The lristory of the trade
during the past year has been such as to
render some such law not only especially
desirable to the lumbermen, but almost
an absolute necessity. They feel that
not only have the roads been partial in
the distribution of favors to the lumber
trade, but that the whole trade has been
j discriminated against. Of course the
roads can set up the defense that many
other kinds of freight must be moved to
prevent absolute loss, while lumber will
1 keep, but the lumbermen have corro to
the conclusion that this idea has been
carried to the extreme, and that they
have not been given a fair proportion of
the rolling stock, even after giving this
argument full consideration.
The socond request is for a law fir the
creation of the office of fire warden and
giving him such powers as will enable
successful work in the prevention of for
est lires. There ought to be no opposi
tion to this. The forests of the fetate are
its in st valuable heritage. The record
of destruction by lire in the ]>ast liat
proven that something of this character
should be done at once. The law should
provide penalties for carelessness on the
part of anyone who operates in the tim
ber. It should demand the utmost care
in the disposal of the refuse left from
logging operations, and should give the
lire warden power enough and money
enough to make his work effective.
The third request is for such changes
in the lite insurance laws of tha state as
will admit of the organization of mutual
companies, i-aw mills are not- the best
of risks, and fires, especially among the
smaller mills of the state, have been
numerous, but the manufacturers do not
believe that the hazard is so great as to
warrant the excessive ta'es now stketl
b/ the old line companies. T.ie rates
are so high 111 many instances as to
nearly, if rot quite annul the margins in
a business that ought to be profitable.
I'he lumbermen believe that they can
save money for themselves if they are
permitted to organize xutual companies.
This effect will be brought about partly
by the saving in rates that will be paid
to the mutual companies, and partly in
the reduction in rates that will surely
follow the enactment of a law that will
indirectly compel a reduction of the
rates asked by the old line concerns
doing business in the state.

JAP A SPY FOR RUSSIA
Traitor to Hii NCountry, He Was
Hacked to Death.
PLOT IS DISCOVERED IN T0KI0
Attempt of Rasslaalsed Japanese to
fleeare Naval Secrete Spy Killed
fcy am Accomplice After II
Had Betrayed Himself.
During: the recent Japanese trouble on
the Pacific coast various person thought
they discovered Japanese . spies taking
measurements and making notes about the
coast defenses thereabouts. The Japanese
War office may have a more or less per
fect system , for gathering Information
about the defenses of other countries, but
It Is a safe guess that It never before had
to Investigate the operations of one of Ja
pan's own people In spying on Japan's own
forts in the interests of a foreign power.
The Tokio Asahl prints an Interesting story
of this unique treaion of a Japanese.
Sejl Mayeda, a former Instructor In the
Oriental ' Languages school of Vladivostok
and a naturalized Russian citlsen. dropped
Into Toklo about two months ago after a
continued absence of over ten years. , He
was highly educated and passed among the
gentlement of the better class In the Toklo
political clubs as a personable man of re
finement. Because he spoke Russian flu
ently and had lived many years In Si
beria If was not considered a matter of
suspicion that Mayeda spent, a great deal
: of his time with Russian military attaches
of the legation.
About the same time that Mayeda came
to Toklo another man from Siberia, Kuzukl
Imamura, came down from the Japanese
fisheries, at Nlkolaevsk, In ' the Frimorsk
province, where he had Ix-en working
since the war. He went to its home In the
slums of the city and nobody knew that
he ever met Mayeda, the gentleman, or that
he knew anything about Mayeda's movo
menta. Reporter oa . His Trail.
On August 9 a Japanese coolie of the low
class visited the office of the Toklo Asahl
and desired a word with the news editor.
When the coolie had that functionary care
fully secluded In an Inner room he told
him that If a reporter from the Asahl
would follow Mayeda, the gentleman and
the clubman, on the train to the Yokosuka
naval station that night he would learn that
the Russlanlsed Japanese was a traitor
and that he was preparing reports of the
national Printing Company
J
LET US OIND YOUR MAGAZINES
Prions Douglas 1008
Ate;
V
defenses at Yokosuka for the Russian gov
ernment.
Maruo, the coolie, told the Asahl editor
that Mayeda had gained his confidence
by hiring him as a servant and that the
day before Mayeda had asked him to take
a night trip to Yokosuka, promising that
for the work he would do there would be
large rewards. Maruo had suspected his
master of being In league with the Russians,
because of his constant association with
the legation staff officers, and this .Yoko
suka trip convinced him, he said, of the
treacherous designs of the man.
Japanese newspapers are not slow at
scenting news even though the reporters
wear clogs and are happy on a salary that
an American newspaper would not think I
of offering to an office boy. The Asahl
Immediately assigned a man to follow
Mayeda and then notified the central office
of the police.
That night when Mayeda and the coolie
went . to Shlmbaahl station to take the
train for Yokosuka an Asahl reporter and
a detective followed. Mayeda thought to
throw possible pursuers off the track by
taking a Bhlmonosekl train and changing
at a junction point beyond Yokohama, but
the reporter and the detective were not
fooled. .
Sleatha Hake Bar.
When Mayeda stopped overnight at an
Inn near Yokosuka the sleuths on his trail
camped there also. The Asahl's account
says they were suitably disguised, but this
probably mean that the two pursuers
muffled their faces with their kimono
sleeves, aa the art of disguise la primitive
In Japan.
The suspect and his Informing servant
spent the night In riotous drinking. The
reporter and the detective crawled Into a
nearby room to hear what Mayeda might
say through the thin paper shoJL He said
enough to convict him.
The next morning when the spy hap
pened to catch sight of the two trailers
he decided that It was getting warm for
htm and tried to double on his tracks
going for a time to the Yoshlwara on the
outskirts of a neighboring town, then
trying to make a quick loop back in the
neighborhood of the forts. All the time
the faithful reporter' and the detective.
faces presumably' still muffled, followed
like shadows.
Mayeda evidently grew suspicious, for
he gave up the trip through the fortifica
tion tone on the following night and re
turned to Toklo. There It was that his
career "of . treason came to a sudden end
before the Intervention of the police.
Kuxukl Imamura, the cootie who bad
known Mayeda In Siberia and who had
come down from the fisheries to live In
the slums of Toklo, had got an Intimation
through some channel that the police be

lieved that he and Mayeda were In the
same plot. On the same day that Mayeda
returned after the fruitless Yokosuka trip
Imamura went to Mayeda's home and
stabbed him a dosen times In the neck
and body with a short sword.
Even when Mayeda tried to escape down
the street the coolie followed, hacking at
him with the sword. Mayeda finally
dropped from exhaustion and soon died.
Imamura gave himself up, saying that he
had done his country a good service In
killing a spy of the Russians.
The police subsequently searched Maye
da's papers and found confirmation of
I heir suspicions that he was trading In
military secrets. They also found evidence
enough to convince them that the patriot
Imamura was In the plot with the gentle
man Mayeda. So Imamura will get the
punishment that Mayeda escaped.
MAKING GODS IN INDIA
As Immense Industry, Loral la Char
acter, Which Is Attracting
Foreigners.
Few of us realize that Into the vast
triangle of Hindustan Is packed one-firth
of the entire human race more than
200,000,000 Hindus, 60,000,000 Moham
medans, 10,000,000 aborigines and well
over 85,000,000 of other miscellaneous
peoples, making up a population of over
800,000,000, 'speaking scores of different
tongues and divided into hundreds of
separate states.
The most Important Industry of India
Is agriculture, for the people are a race
of farmers, and nearly two-thirds of the
masses cultivate the soil, eking out a
living so scanty that the slightest fail
ure of -the monsoon brings acute distress,
If not positive famine.
It Is perhaps for this reason that India
Is the most god-ridden region on earth.
Its deities are numbered In millions,
for quite apart from the greater god,
every little hamlet between the tre
mendous Himalayas and Cape Comorln
has its own set of deities, dreadful and
beneficent.
From this It will be seen that goJ-
maklng In India must necessarily be an
Immense business, and just now there is
much feeling among the native artificers
over this holy and profitable industry
being cut Into by foreign merchants and
traders. Only the other day an enormous
five-tiered Juggernaut car . of gaily
painted wood and steel was made in
Calcutta, and of late years Birmingham
and Philadelphia have both secured big
slices of the traffic In gods.
Every village, especially In south India,
Is supposed to be surrounded by evil
spirits, always on the watch to inflict
disease and misfortune on the people. At
the . same time every little hamlet has
also . Its guardian spirits who ward off
the evil ones and protect the villagers
from epidemics of cholera, smallpox, cat
tle diseases, famine and all. the dire aud
m-ifold Ills that Indian flesh is heir to.
The names of these village deities are
quite unintelligible to the people them
selves. On the other hand, many of
them have meanings which show clearly
their close connection with country life.
Thus the "Village Goddess," the "Great
Mother." the "Water Goddess," the
"Goddess Who Presides Over Butter
milk," the "Goddess Who Sits Under a
Mango Tree" aud so on. In the Tamil
country Marl-amman, the goddess of
smallpox, both inflicts and chases away
this dread disease.
Sometimes there la no permanent Image
or symbol of a village deity, but a spe
cial clay efflgy a couple of feet high ia
made for each god festival by the village
potter. Again, the deity may be repre
sented by a rough stone pillar standing
under a tree or In the open field, Ofrby
the figure of a woman carved la high
relief upon a stone slab. Hw Tork

AWFUL CURSE OF COCAINE
l'e of the Deadly Dra araadtnST
and Even Children Are
Victims.
Aa Item In the news columns of one of
the New Tork dallies records the at
tempted suicide of a boy of 16. It la
probable that this boy will die; one can
hardly wish that he should live. Indeed,
for the cause of his effort to end his
life was the cocaine habit. It seems
that In the boy's home, Newark, N. J.,
this veritable curse has reached such
proportions that a crusade has been or
ganized to stamp out the Illegal selling
or use of the drug. Over 100 arrests have
already been made, and this unfortunate
lad undoubtedly feared public disgrace
If he did not put an and to a ruined life.
His relatives had made every effort to
cure him of the fatal habit; but no mesne
could be found to rob the cocaine of Its
fascination for the boy. At the time
when he was found half dead several
boxes "of "Cocaine were In his potirtis.-
life
Sts.
reached a point where cocaine should be
recognised as actually a national menace.
In China the government under luap
Shih-Kal is malting a steady and deter
mined fight against the opium habit. In
this country the cocaine habit univers
ally recognized by physicians as the most
dangerous of all artificial means of stim
ulating or deadening the faculties is
gaining headway at a rate so rapid that
those who have studied the situation
hesitate to predict the probable results.
Not alone among ,the white race, but
among the negroes also has the cocaine
habit reached alarming proportions. A
writer in one of the current periodicals
relates a case In which the negroes work
ing in a certain place were systematically
led Into debauchery by a fiend in human
shape a white man who Induced them
to use cocaine and thus reduced them
from fairly efficient workers to idlers
or brutes, according to their natural ten
dencies. The effect of cocaine Is acknowledged
to be, without exception, pernicious when
used for any purpose other than a strictly
therapeutic one. It debases the mind and
morals much more, quickly than opium,
and Us effect en the vital forces Is almost
Immediate. Deterioration of every part
of the bodily system Is the penalty for
Its use. No possible excuse -for employ
ing Its agency without a physician's or
der can be found. And yet In spite of
all these things, In spite of the new laws
by which all so-called "medicines con
taining cocaine must be plainly labelel,
the scourge Is spreading. Without doubt
we have to thank these "medicines" for
a good part of the evlL Many persons

Inefficiency Is not a local evil. It
extends through the whole of Amer
ican life extends through the
whole industrial life of the world,
says a writer In the Engineering
Magazine. The Chinese coolie, who
as a daily task carries lt9 pounds
27 miles for 27 rents, is Industrious
and hardworking, but not more in
efficient than the American railroad
which moves a freight car an aver
age of 23 miles a day, the cars at
best, averaging only half loads per
mile. By a very Inefficient use of
his brain and muscles, the coolie
carries the maximum load a maxi
mum distance for a minimum price.
The American railroad by the most
advanced engineering and industrial
methods carries an absurdly small
set load for an absurdly small dis
tance at an unnecessarily hiah cost.
Prevailing lnfficiency is not a lapse
from former virtue. We annot
praise "the good old times" when
everything was done better. The
coolie In pl of his many virtue;
Is not better than the railroad who
rharges per ton mile average only
one-thIrtie-h those of the coolie.
The difference is, however, that ele
mentary though his methods are,
the coolie has hich s'andards.
evolved during many cnturi". hut

road and the modern shop, using

The steel trust has very reluc
tantly agreed to reducs the price of
its products in obedience to that in
exorable law of supply and de
mand. That the cut !n prices Is
neither wide nor de?p enough to I
satisfy many buyers, will probably
lead to the producers outside of the
trust, making lower prices than j
those agreed upon and then the
trust will have to agree to still
lower prices. No change was made
in the price of steel rails, which are
more nearly monopolized than any
other steel product. It is evident
that If ths steel trust had a com
plete monopoly of steel products
there would have been no reduction
in prices, but the spirit and competi
tion not being wholly extinguished
even the steel trust could not suf
ficiently control the steel industry
to keep some of the independent
companies from cutting prices, and,
therefore, the great trust has had
to yield to the Inevitable. Does not
this example plainly show the value
of competition in keeping not only
the steel trust but all the trusts
from overhanging. As the general
trend here is for the trusts to gather
in their smaller competitors and
then there would be complete mo
nopoly, unless the tariff rates are
reduced or abolished so as to bring
in competition from abroad. As
it is now under the present tariff,
the rates are so high that but few
manufactured articles can be im
ported to compete with the trusts.
Revising the tariff by adding max
imum rates and making , the pres
ent rates the minimum, which !s
said to be the republican plan of
revision, would only-maintain trust
monopoly and add to present prices
on products from those countries
with which no reciprocity treaty ex
isted. Before you vote this fall
look into both sides of this." tariff
issue, and then decide whether the
stand pat policy, or real tariff re
form, will best suit your interests.

TO DEDICATE NEW CHAPEL.
Several Ansonians Expect to Attend
Ceremonies at Crescent Beach.
Ansonla, iune 27. The dedica
tion of the new St Francis chapel
a' Crescent Beach, two weeks from
Sunday, will probably be .attended
by a good Bized delegation of, An
sonlans. Rev Father Nolan, the
pastor of the church. Is personally
known to a number of people in
this city. He is a young man of
high attainments, a One speaker,
and one of the most earnest work
ers In the church. Father Nolan,
who Is about 28 years old. Is a grad
uate or Yale. 1900S., and Immediate
ly after entered the employ of the
New York, New Haven and Hart
ford railroad.
For two or three years he was
engaged in railroad work, planning
and superintending the new layout
between this city and Derby. Dur
ing this time he continued his
studies and later went to Europe,
where he completed his education.
He was ordained to the priesthood
two years ago, and was immediate
ly appointed to fill the chair of
science at St Thomas' seminary.
Besides teaching the sciences and
building churches. Father Nolan li
engaged In missionary work among
the Italian population of the diocese,
being a fluent and accomplished
Italian speaker. His summer months
are spent at Crescent Beach, where
he has gathered a large congrega
tion, and at Sound - View, where a
chapel was erected two years ago.

Relleview, July 3. The niht of
June 25th Jdpro Hopkins loaned a
Ions rope to an automobile party to
tow a disabled car to Ocala. The one
that borrowed the rope promised faith
fully to return the rope that night,
sayins; he had to return to Lake Weir
that night. As yet the rope has not
been returned. The judge has con
ferred a number of favors on auto
people and is sorry to see that the one
he loaned the rope to is so unappre

drivers. O. M. Gale has lost two ropes

feels that he is through loaning ar

the dishonest ones.

Davenport. Then followed a social
time in which gentlemen and ladies

evening there was a good attendance.

l'roctor, fraternal master; J. D. Lyles.

dies are eligible to membership.

Ark.; Miss Ella T.yles. to Dunnellon,

South Carolina; Mrs. Clara Haines and
Freeman Haines to GafTney, South
Carolina.

gan to chatter and spoke at length,
delivering himself of a long state-,
ment which Couch punctured now and
again with pertinent, leading ques
tions. At length, throwing aside the knife,
he Jumped up, strong excitement burn
ing in his eyes. "I've got enough from
him," he said rapidly. "I'll explain
later. YouH help of course; your
wife's involved as well as Miss Pyn
sent. But I don't think you need fear;
we'll be in time. Are ycu ready? .
. . Half a minute; I've got to use
that telephone."
He ran out Into the hall, rang up
and shouted a number Into the re
ceiver, and for a few moments spoke
rapidly in a Burmese ( dialect.
O'Rourke gathered that he was speak
ing with a native subordinate at the
police headquarters In Rangoon.
Couch swung back Into the study,
"Got those revolvers, sir? Then come
along; we'll have to run for It For
tunately our launch Is handy; other
wise ..."
He sprang across the veranda and
down to the lawn, O'Rourke pelting
after him.
(Continued Tomorrow)

THE PACIFIC COMMERCIAL ADVERTISER, HONOLULU, JUNE 27, 1901.
THE PACIFIC
Commercial Advertiser
CTAIAEB O. SMITH
- EDITOR.
WEDNESDAY
JUNE 27
The Legislature Is getting into the
same boot-poised attitude towards
Humphreys which the Bar Association
assumed with such deadly effect.
4
Would the organs that are trying to
laugn down the annexation conspiracy
now that they see what effect it is hav
ing on the Hawaiian vote, like the name
of the Home Rule leader whom Tom
Fitch asked to join it?
A reporter of the Advertiser was un
able yesterday to find a Home Rule
member of the Legislature who did not
Bay that the Advertiser's story of the
Mumphreys-Fitch annexation deal was
4tlrely correct. All of them are in a
position to know.
The sinking of the ferry boat North
field in New York Bay, costing some
lives, recalls the fact that her sister
craft, the Sotithfield, blew up in her
dock many years ago and killed 300
passengers. If the third member of the
trio, the Westfield, is still running, peo
ple will want to take out an insurance
policy before they go aboard.
The death of former Qovernor Pin
gree of Michigan removes a man whoso
scheme for raising potatoes on the va
cant Iota of Detroit, by and for the
homeless and poor, first made him fam
ous. Plngree was a man of great inde
pendence of character and no tact,
qualities that circumscribed his success
as a politician. But a multitude of de
feats were covered by his honesty.
THE YELLOW PERIL
Already the lines are being drawn for
the contest which is to mark the next
session of Congress, the fight for the re
enactment of the Chinese exclusion law.
The Geary exclusion law was passed
nineteen years ago, and will expire in
May, 1902. It .was in December, 1884,
that the Gresham treaty with China,
which embodies the consent of the lat
ter nation to the terms at. the act was
signed and promulgated.
During the last term of Congress
.there was ""continual agitation of the
matter, especially by Congressman
Kaon of San Francisco, who had ready
' for introduction a bill which would
continue the exclusion act's force for
another twenty years. That he did not
introduce it, according to the reports in
the papers of his home city, was due
to the fact that it was deemed wisest
to allow the matter to rest until the
long term of Congress, when there
would be final action on the matter.
By this plan there would be no double
working up of sentiment necessary.
From the two extremes of the conti
rtent come tales of the activity of those
interested in the exclusion law. In
Mew York an organization has been
effected on the part of the Chinese,
which has for its object the prepara
tion of a memorial to the Government
protesting against a re-enactment of
the law. It is set forth that each one
of the thousands of Chinese In the
United Spates will be asked to sign
this mamccial. and it is conceivable
that there will be collected a fund for
the proper presentation of the claims
of the protestants, which will attract
the attention of one of the largest lob
bies which has been seen at the. na
tional capital for many years. The
York" Chinese. Hon. will as' all
hoep of the Americans : who hav not
been oppoesd to the opening of; the
doors to the Orientals, to sign a mem
orial which will be made a feature of
the fight.
Meanwhile, on the Western coast,
where the agitation against the flood of
cheap labor had its beginning, " the
fight Is already being put up. Labor
organizations and newspapers have .be
gun to agitate for the pledging of mem
bers of Congress to a new law which
will stem the yellow scourge. The
friends of white labor, the unions of
artisans in all lines of human endeavor,
the men who think upon the subject
broadly, all are in unison on this mat
ter. There is nothing to be done but to
secure a longer period of relief from
the danger of 'the extinction of the
American' workman - as he now exists,
the best paid, the highest living work
map in the world, and this can be done
only by the absolute prohibition of the
entrance of Chinese labor into the
United States. The same feeling, which,
when fully aroused, brought the people
of the United States to see the threat
ening danger of the yellow cloud, has
been displayed in some extent In the
West In regard to the little brown men,
the so-called Yankee of the Pacific, the
Japanese. It Is a fact that within the
past five years there has been develop
ed In many Western States as strong
an antipathy to the Jap as is felt to
ward the coolie.
The danger which lies In the way of
the re-enactment of exclusion legisla
tion is that there shall be among leg
islators from the East an absolute lack
of knowledge bearing upon the condi
tions which will obtain when the Or
iental Is given a free field to compete
with the free white man. In various
Eastern newspapers there have ap
peared in recent years a criticism of the
course of the West In demanding
more stringent restriction laws. At
one time the New York Sun
served notice upon the West
that there was absolutely no hope of se
curing any restriction of Japanese Im
migration, yet we have seen that flood
stayed by the Emperor himself. It is
in the spreading of Information as to
the scourge which the yellow man
proves, that the widest field for the
proponents of the exclusion laws now
lies. The fact that every national labor
union has taken up this branch of the
service gives hope that there will be no
member of either House of the national
Legislature who will have the question
brought to his notice and yet be unpre
pared to vote upon It for the best Inter
ests of the entire people of the country.
The greatest hope of those who be
friend the yellow man is that the pop
ularity of the present Minister from
China to Washington will enable him
to so divide the forces as to make It
Impossible for a bill to be put Into
shape for passage. Minister Wu Ting
Fang has made great stride into the
good graces of Americans. For the
first time since the establishment of
diplomatic relations between the two
countries Is the feeble empire of Asia
represented at the capital of this na
tion by a man who has a conception, of
the people to whom he was sent, and
who has taken the trouble to make him
self known to their.. Minister Wu
has taken all pains W show that he is
one of the progressive and thinking
men of the day. This will go far, but
not far enough. It will be found that
there is a vast difference between ap
plause for a clear and pleasant speaker
and a logical thinker, and support for
his side of a controversy which has to
do with the vital interests of the- rank
and file of the American people. The
time is not yet when the coolie may be
allowed to enter into competition with
the Anglo-Saxon. American workmen
have become the most capable a:ad in
telligent through the enormous advant
ages which are theirs through high
wages and prosperous conditions. The
front rank which is conceded to Ameri
can trade could not be gained and held
but with the highest grade of workmen,
and the recent investigations have
shown that the American workman,
educated and thoughtful, can produce
more than any competitor. Debase this
freedom by bringing him into competi
tion with an imitative coolie, who can
live upon the sum paid by the former
for his luxuries, and there will be a
passing of the advantages which have
been joined by years of specializing
training.
The argument is heiner used that there
are vast areas in the West which will
not be improved under the systems
which obtain. That there are lands to
be prepared for cultivation. Irrigating
ditches to be built and roads to be con
structed, which cannot be made if the
labor is to be paid at the rates which
the white freeman must have. True it
is that for this kind of work the coolie
or peon is of value. But there has not
been devised any plan by which the
cheap laborer can be kept in the condi
tion for which he is needed. Here in
Hawaii the Inroads, Which have been
made in the trades by the Chinese who
came here a decade ago as a contract
laborer, are apparent. The same will
be the result In larger measure in the
States after a while: that It has not
been so already, is due to the special
duties which have engaged the atten
tion of the coolie. The yellow flood must
be held In check, and the agitation now
on promises to insure such result.
ECONOMY IN POLES.
With the stringing of the wires for
the overhead traction system of the
Rapid Transit Company, there becomes
apparent what may be termed an abuse
of privileges, for which a remedy is at
hand, and one that would appear to of
fer the double advantages of cheapness
and desirability. It is absolutely neces
sary where there is to be overhead elec
tric system that there be set poles for
the suspension of the trolley wires.
These poles are set in palra generally,
on the opposite side of a street, at reg
ular intervals. Irrespective of the pres
ence of telephone or lighting wires.
To illustrate: On Wilder avenue
where the work of setting the poles and
stringing the trolley wires for the elec
tric road is complete, the telephone and
light companies have their wires strung
on independent sets of poles. These,
with the necessary poles of the railroad,
are often placed In immediate prox
imity. In more than one Instance along
that thoroughfare there may be found
three poles within a distance of fifteen
feet. This means that the obstructions,
the opportunities for disfigurement are
multiplied by three, for there appears
no really good reason why there might
not be arrangements by which the
wires of the three corporations should
be placed on the poles which support
the trolleys of the railroad.
The street railroad wires are placed
at the height of twenty-two feet, while

The Ozar Was a Lazy Youngster.
The Czar, when a youth, showed a
tendency to lassitude, which pointed
to a want of physical stamina. This
disinclination to physical exertion
showed itself when he was visiting the
late Queen at Balmoral, and the royal
keepers were a little chagrined At the
lack of enthusiasm which he displayed
with regard to the deer stalking ar
ranged for his diversion.
Many Counterfeit Pennies.
From $3,000 to $6,000 In pennies " is
daily received by Uncle Sam's New
York agents in Wall Street, and hun
dreds of them are spurious. The open
ing of the slot machine season is blam
ed for this sudden increase in "queer"
coin. The slot machines are constant
losers by "blank" or bad coin, which
is made of correct weight and thick
ness, and escapes detection by the ma
chine. Most of the fractional coin 're
ceived at the subtreasury is paid out
to large department stores, which have
use for "chicken feed" money. Some
of the big stores get as much as $10,000
in pennies at one time and haul them
from the vaults in Wall Street like so
much junk. Secret service men aire
scouring New York at present look
ing for the illegal "mint"' which is en
gaged in turning out spurious pennies
in wholesale quantities.

Will Tackle Assimboin&'s Peak
Edward Whymper, the British
mountain climber, intends to spend the
coming summer with Swiss guides
among the Rocky mountains of Cana
da. He will endeavor to ascend a num
ber of peaks that have not yet been
climbed, and he has particularly in
view Mount Assiniboine, a fine peak
about twenty miles south of the Cana
dian Pacific Railroad. It is nearly 12,
000 feet high, bears a remarkable re
semblance to the Matterhorn, and is
apparently inaccessible on all sides.
Several attempts to ascend this moun
tain have failed.
KeptgHiB Promise to His Mother.
While his molher lay dead at her
home in Columbus, O., Col. A. B. Coit
delivered a Memorial Day address to
2.000 people at Delaware. He was
chosen orator of the day some time
ago, but after he had written a speech
his mother was taken seriously ilL
On the evening before Decoration Mrs.
Coit called her son to her bedside and
requested that he read the speech to
her. After the colonel had finished his
mother said in a faint whisper: "That
is a good speech, my son. Now prom
ise me that no matter how ill I am
you will not disappoint your Delaware
audience." The colonel gave his word
as requested, and though his mother
died before morning he delivered the
address according to the program.
Will Devote Her Life to Lepers.
Mrs. Laura Schwlchtenberg, a weal
thy widow of New York, has decided
to devote her life to our new leper col
ony on the island of Cebu, one of the
Philippine group. Some time ago she
received, at her own urgent request.
appointment as government Inspector
of hospitals in the Philippines, at
which time she visited the leper colo
ny, declaring that her commission
took that in. as th whole colony was
a hospital. She was greatly impressed
with the lack of sanitary conditions
prevailing, with the hopeless condition
of the 30,000 lepers congregated there
and especially with the large number
of sad children. "I did not see a single
happy-faced child there, she says
"The scenes still haunt me. I long to
return and take a little sunshine into
their lives."

Noticing two tents In the distance,
Knubol left his companions and rode
off alone to see who were the occu
pants of the tents. Pulling aside the
flap of the first one, he saw a bundle of
children's . clothing and several wo
men's garments. As he withdrew and
was about to turn around his gaze fell
upon a man leaving the other tent,
w horn he recognized as Jefferson Davis.
A sharp "halt!" and the head of the
Confederacy knew that his flight was
ended. Discharging his musket as a
signal to his comrades, the young cav
alryman advanced, and, placing his
hands on the rebel chief's shoulder,
told him he was a prisoner.
After the war Knobel located In this
city, and is now living at No. 2703 East
Cumberland street, and is an active
member of Cavalry Post No. 5 . Phila

text:
The other day I was talking to a
very intelligent man who has bad
many opportunities of meeting globe
trotters—globe trotters for reasons of
business rather than of pleasure—and
he told me a remarkable story which
had been told to him by one of these
globe trotters. The story throws a
significant light on the reasons of the
extraordinary success the Japanese
have been gaining in their recent bat
tles.
A Japanese trader was leaving Port
Arthur just an It becamtfWident that
war must come, lie had several
trunks, and these he entrusted to a
Chinese ccolie, who seemed to be well
Up to his work, and who handled the
trunks with the characteristic prompti
tude and facility of a Chinaman of
that class. When the Japan.se got on
board he missed his coolie, and he
found among his baggage another
trunk which he did not recognize. He
thought nothing more of the affair,
believing it to be one of the mistakes
which were inevitable in a time of
bury and something like panic. Rut
when the vessel in which he was trav

Army Drums Hauled by Dogs.
A curious feature connected with the
Servian army is the manner in which
most of the regiments carry the big
drum. It is not, as in most countries,
slung In front of the man who plays
it, but is placed upon a small two
wheeled cart drawn by a single dog,
which has been so trained that it keeps
its place even through the longest and
most tedious of marches. The drum
mer takes up a position behind the cart
and performs on the instrument as the
animal pulls it along.

Value of Their Various Crops Thia Year
Will Reach $3,21)0.00,0060.
Much lies been said and printed about
the failure of the wheat crop this year.
The yield was under that fi’st expected,
and under the yields of recent years.
But the extraordinarily high prices for
wheat prevailing this fall have made the
ctcp probably the most valuable in the
history of the country to the fanners. It
is estimated that the wheat yield this
year was Werth to the farmers about
$551,000.030, as compared with a value
ol: $443,000,000 last year. $422,200,000
in 1902, and $467,300,000 in 1901.
The cotton crop of the present year
is close to the best in size and quality
ever produced in this country. The spec
ulation in cotton hist year, however, made
M extremely high range of prices for the
staple and probably put the total value
ol' last season's crop to the planters at
a higher figure than will be realized for
the crop this season. The estimated fig
ures are $600,000,000 for this year’s
crop, as compared with $617,500,000 for
last year’s crop, $480,700,000 in 1902,
am! $438,000,000 in 1901.
The hay crop is one of the most val
uable that the farmers of the country
raise, although little is heard of it and
its vicissitudes during the growing sea
son. This year the hay crop is very
large, but the price is not up to the av
erage of former years. The total value
of the crop is estimat<>d at $550,000,000,
as compared with $556,800,000 in 1903,
$342,000,000 in 1902, and $506,100,000 in
1901.
The potato crop this year is one of the
best, if not the best, In the history of
the country, and although the price per
bushel is considerably less than that pre
vailing last year, the remuneration to the
farmers will be larger. There is indi
cated total value of the potato crop
of $158,000,000, as compared with $151,-
600,000 last year, $134,100,000 in 1902,
and $143,900,000 in 1901.
The oat, rye, barley and buckwheat
crops are excellent this year, and prices
are good. The yields to the farmers this
fall are figured at a total of $356,000,000
for the four crops, as compared with
$352,200,000 last year, $390,900,000 in
1902 and $368,700,000 in 1901.
Combined, the crops of the country
this year represent' a value to the farm
ers, at the prevailing prices, larger than
ever before in the history of the country.
The grand total of the yield of the har
vests this fall is estimated at $3,200,-
000,000. Last year records were broken
by a yield of $3,073,000,000. In 1902 the
harvest was worth $2,987,000,000. in
191 the yield to the farmers was $2,-
845.000,000.
COTTON IS KING AGAIN.
Conies to Its Own Once More as the
Monarch of Our Crops.
Despite the ravages of the boll weevil,
and despite, also, the ups and downs of
the speculators in the cotton market,
cotton, that most useful product of the
sunny South, is at the front again as the
monarch of American crops. Exports of
cotton and its products from the United
States exceed by millions the value of
any other single crop sent out. and dur
ing the fiscal year ending June brought
into this country more than $400,000,000.
The exports have very nearly doubled
in a decade, and in the last year they
have increased by more than $40,000,000.
I’roducts of cotton other than the raw
fiber which go to make up this groat total
are cottonseed oil, cottonseed meal, cot
ton waste, cotton seed in the natural
state, and cotton manufactures, (jotton
seed meal and cottonseed oil as factors
in export trade are of recent develop
ment. Exports of both of these amount
.now to more than $25,000,000 annually,
while only a very short time ago the seed
from which these are manufactured was
considered almost worthless. The value
of cottonseed oil exported from this
country last year was more than $14,000,-
000. Of oil cake and oil-cake meal ex
ported from the United States last year,
amounting to nearly $20,000,000, $4,500,-
000 worth went to Germany, $4,000,000
to Belgium, $3,500,000 to the Nether
lands, and a little less than $3,500,000
to the United Kingdom. All this means
prosperity for the South, better homes,
better schools, and more happiness and
contentment all around for her people.

RODE FOR HER LIFE.
Thrilling Experience of n Montana
Girl with Wild Dog*.
Chased for more than a tulle by a
j ack of wild dogs, apparently a cross
between the common dog and the
wolf. Miss Gertie Sullivan, daughter
of a sheepman living on Birch creek,
nineteen miles from Havre. Mont., es- 1
caped only’ by spending the night in
an old corral and picking off the ani
mals now and then with rifle bullets.
Miss Sullivan was riding her pany
across the prairie when she saw some
distance away, perhaps twenty ani
mals, which at first glance she took
to be coyotes, says the St. Paul Pioneer
Press.
As they came nearer, evidently In
pursuit of. her, the girl saw they were
not coyotes, but wild dogs, of which
there are several small packs in north
ern Montana and just over the Cana
dian line.
With the bruti'S hot on her trail. It
became a tide for life with Miss Sulli
van. She had with her a small rifle,
but the animals seemed so savage she
dartsl not stop and give them battle.
As she began to despair of saving
her life she sighted the old sheep cor
ral. and toward this she ran her horse,
reaching it only when the pack was a
short distance away, snapping and
yelling with rage.
To enter with the pony and quickly
close the gate behind her was the
work of a moment. As the gate swung
into place the leaders of the pack
threw themselves against it.
Snapping and snarling with anger,
the dogs tore at the poles forming
the stockade, but were unable to gain
an entrance into the place of refuge.
After recovering her composure and
finding that she was safe, the girl be
gan shooting at the animals, which re
treated when she fired and returned to
the attack when the rifle was silent.
Miss Sullivan was found by her
father and her brother, who had been
seeking her. The dog pack disappeared
when the men approached. The girl
had killed several of the animals.
Ills Caused by Sunlight.
While the rays of the sun possess a
stimulating and beneficial effect in
disease and can be used with advan
tage as a therapeutic agent, yet, ac-
cording to recent investigations, they
also may act injuriously and cause dis
ease. That there is a difference in
the therapeutic value between the ac
tinic rays, or those located at the violet
end of the spectrum, and the red rays
was appreciated by Finsen. and the
former were used in the treatment of
such skin disease's as lupus vulgaris,
or tuberculosis of the skin, while the
latter were found effective In cases of
smallpox.
One of the most recent researches
in this department is by Prof. Fermi
and is given in a late number of the
Arehiv fur Hygiene. In these experi
ments a large number of human sub
jects were exposed to direct sunlight
for varying periods, and in a large per
centage of cases it was found that
there resulted the following set of
symptoms: Cephalagia (headache), dry
ness of the nasal mucosa, snuffling,
coryza (cold in the head), pharyngitis,
weariness, slight conjunctivitis, dry
ness of the lips, fever, pseudo influenza,
constipation, insoinuia, epistaxis (nose
bleed) and various pains. From this
list of symptoms experienced by his
patients and from the observed coinci
dence of certain diseases and meteor
ological conditions Prof. Fermi con
cludes that exposure to the sun’s rays
is a predisposing factor in coryza, in
fluenza, hay fever and epidemic menin
gitis.—Harper’s Weekly.
How Koryak Children Travel.
Koryak women of Northwestern Si
beria, having no carriages in which
to convey their offspring, resort to the
ingenious method of dropping the tiny
creatures into blanket bags which are
tightly strapped about the mothers’
necks.

The Daisy.
In French the daisy is called la
Marguerite. It was the device of Mar
guerite of Anjou, and also of Marguer
ite of Valois, a much more appropriate
emblem of the letter princess, who
withdrew from the glitter of courts to
become a reeluse, than of the ambi
tious Lancastrian queen of England.
The daisy is the national flower of'
Italy, chosen in honor of Queen Mar- i
guerite. In the language of flowers |
it signifies innocence, peace, hope. In ;
the age of chivalry It was the emblem
of fidelity and worn by knights at tour
naments in honor of their lady loves. I

Man-Eaters Guard Trail Of Emerald Buddha
Death Hovers Near As Capt’n Bill’s
Party Pursues Quest Of Sacred Idol
SYNOPSIS
THE Captain of the “Mary Ann”
tell* the tale of how his mate.
Bill Corkey, meets a Ternate
dancing girl who gives him a map
revealing the hiding place of the
fabulous Emerald Buddha in
Angkor Wat, a carved idol sought
by the pirate. Sin Kew. They es
oape from Sin Kew's men to sail
for Saigon. Word of their arrival
has come by fast junk, and a
Frenchman meets them at the
dock. They visit Huii Doon.
money lender, who owes his life
to the Captain, and promises his
help. Huli Doon’s granddaughter
serves rice wine, and later the
two men take a room at the
Hotel Continent, where they bathe
and rest. Returning to Huii
Doon’s. they find a pack horse,
fresh clothes, food and rifle ready
for them--and a coolie guide to
take them through the jungle
northward toward Angkor Wat.
The money lender gives them a
message for Li, a Buddhist priest
living near the ruined Khmer
temples.
By Whit Wellman
Chapter 5
NO! Our friend Sin Kew had
special plans for us at the
end of the trail. He’d gone to a
lot of trouble in Ternate and
Saigon to get Bill's parchment
map, and the pirate was the kind
to finish what he'd begun. At
Angkor he'd have time to prepare
a welcome, even wait until we'd
located the sealed room. It would
be easier for him. that way. When
I thought about it, nothing could
be wiser for Sin Kew—just to let
us go ahead and do the work,
while he waited.
There was time to think, to
wonder about Sin Kew and his
odd determination about a carved
bit of emerald he couldn't prove
existed. The hot breath of the
jungle clung to us as we pushed
ahead: a thick roof of branches
and twisting lianas hung over us
day and night. The forest was
an unfriendly place, mostly si
lence with occasional bird cries
and gibbons coming to life, chat
tering at us. Gloomy and dark, it
