didn’t care if we lived or died,
whether we came out from its
canopy of dusk or stumbled to
death in deep beds of rotting
ferns. Yes, time to decide what
you'd do in Sin Kew’s place—
how two foreigners in a strange
country could be conveniently
dropped into the hereafter.
Huii Doon had warned. "I think
you will be sad—"
THE money lender knew too
much. How had wo.rd come
so swiftly to Saigon ? By fast
junk, passing us in the night?
That was possible, for those ships
sailed faster than most. Why did
Huii Doon know about us, and
what had the Hotel Continent
heard? From the time Bill danced
with the girl in Ternate. nothing
but trouble. A girl, a parchment
map. jumping from that uncom
fortable hotel room to Ternate’s
street, dashing for the ship. Sin
Kew's men were everywhere:
aboard the Mary Ann, in Saigon
where the bull elephant almost
trampled us. That last was
strange.lt had happened just after
we'd left the money lender. He
could have sent word, and planned
the attack. I tried to tie a single
thread through these hazardous
events. It came to this: my friend
Huii Doon was mixed up in it.
He’d helped us, but had not
wanted us to stay in Saigon. His
interest—l didn’t like to believe
it—could be a mask for deeper
motives. My friend because of
obligation, he might be Sin Kew's
friend as well. With a debt to the
pirate which must be paid ?
If that was so, and old Huii
Doon had some interest in the
green go<j. it might explain
things. Young Bill, for one. He'd
been lookin' at our coolie from
the corner of his eye. In that
dark tangle of forest he couldn’t
see the boy very clear: we did
well to see a few feet ahead.
Now an' then the sun got through
in early morning when we slept,
but mostly neither sun nor moon
did any good. It was dark. The
trail we stumbled along was soft
an’ wet. unpleasant footing for
sailors used to a hard deck. You
didn't complain, but you couldn't
cheer.
I noticed Huii Gim didn’t walk
close if he could help it; sort of
hung behind, following. He
wouldn't talk much, but when
words came, it was educated
English. I'd been too busy avoid
in' creepers and logs that dropped
along our path to bother about
the little guide. Whatever Bill
was thinkin’, he had no chance
to tell me; we weren't alone ever,
but always that coolie close
enough to hear.
BILL cussed at the vines a lot.
then broke off to sing a
rowdy mumbling song to him
self. And without his sayin’ any
thing, I saw him get more curious
about Gim.
We went slowly, too slow to
suit me. The horse got tired in
the heat, I guessed; then I won
dered if it was the animal or the
guide who kept the pace to what
a snail could do. I wasn't for
gettin’ the money lender had sup
plied that coolie without our
askin’. I wasn't afraid, just puz
zled. We wouldn’t'get our parched
throats cut, for the boy carried
no knife, at least none that
showed. An’ Bill—l knew Bill
Corkey guessed somethin’ I hadn’t
seen.
Seemed like we’d keep on for
ever. The jungle got no thinner,
and my temper didn’t improve
any. You get tired of rice, an'
plenty sick of strollin’ between
moss-coated trees that haven’t
been dry since before you were
born. Aye, longer, maybe. No
man knew, an’ I was one of mil
lions who didn't care.
Near as you could tell, it was
midnight when Gim’s black pa
jamas crept up beside me. an’
his small hand caught my arm.
“Pause, please. We are here —”
His husky voice called to bill.
"We shall stop, please.”
Bill swung 'round, sweating; he
was willing to stop.
"What for?”
I couldn’t tell him, and looked
down at Gim's face. The mouth
was drawn up, figuring some
thing. He waved us back, and
moved up in front of Bill.
"Li is near, where we stay. You
will await me. This bonze does
not like strangers.” Huii Gim
stepped around a coco palm and
disappeared into the damp wall
of jungle. We heard soft foot
steps, then nothing. Our talk had
waked a gibbon overhead, and
the beast chattered at us.
“Guess we’re deserted,” I said.
"Not that," Bill muttered, "but
there's somethin’ unnatural about
that coolie.”
"Such as what, Bill? I’ve seen
you watchin’ him.”
“Too quiet, doesn't want to
talk. Just eats an’ sleeps an’
walks quiet-like through this
cursed forept. Why—”
But the guide had come back
noiselessly.
with me, please. Li is
Twenty yards around that coco
palm we saw a torch set over a
rock grotto. Beside the orange
flame an ugly lookin’ carved
snake's head hung down, an' the
waving fire tossed long shadows
of it sideways into the trees. We
didn't see any bonze.
“Come with me.” said Huii
Gim. going under the blazin’
torch
I saw something, then. A grin
nin' tiger's head, swaving rest
lessly in the dim opening. Our
hairless horse smelled it. too. and
snorted. I began to smell what he
did, a strong animal odor, stuffy
an’ not pleasant. Wild tiger.
"rVYOU see it, Bill?" I could
L J swear it's the same beast
that followed us.
Bill got his rifle up. This time
he pulled the trigger. The animal’s
head vanished into that black pool
of hidden cave.
The shot echoed somewhere inside,
an' Bill grunted.
“Might have known I’d miss
him. He's not natural, I tell you.
Is this fellow Li inside that fool
place?”
"We must not stand here, w Huii
Gim urged, “This Lord Tiger be
longs to Li, it will not harm us.”
"Damned if T w-piir fritr. a Wank
Ihe Towers of Angkor Wat. Somewhere Within the Sacred
Precincts of This Ancient Temple Is Hidden the Emerald
Buddha.
hole with that striped pet waitin'.
Not without a light!" Bill looked
up at the snake's head, saw it
wasn't anything but curling stone,
an' pulled down the flarin’ torch.
It sputtered in his hand and threw
sparks around. "Take it, Captain
—l’ll bring the horse and Huii
Gim."
Holding it high. I led the way
into the strangest place we’d
stumbled on. The smell got worse.
Parts of the torch burned off and
splashed to the wet ground; it
wouldn’t last long, so we hurried
down the long passage. When
you hurry, it’s hard to see much
—but each side of that tunnel
was cut deep with scrolls and pic
ture writing, worn an’ polished by
weather, some of it glazed a dark
red.
THEN the tiger smell faded. We
came out into a high ceilinged
room. It was odd, but there
seemed to be two layers of air,
one high overhead very dense;
you could see sharp rocks jut
ting down through it, not cafved,
but the natural formation of the
grotto. Where we walked, the air
was clear. You could see clear
across the length of the place,
near to eighty feet. The floor was
smooth, with slabs of red sand
stone laid close together. Across
the space sat a great Buddha,
reaching to the ceiling where its
head was almost lost in the
gloom. It looked down on us,
with arms folded peacefully, a
half smile on its big face. Around
it flickered fat wax candles, giv
ing more light than any candles
I’d seen. I caught the odor of
heavy incense like crushed Chi
nese orchids drifting from a round
brass bowl beside the statue.
A young bonze in a yellow silk
robe stood before the Buddha, fac
ing us.
"Li,” Huii Gim murmered, “I
have brought them.”
The Chinese didn’t say any
thing. He spread out smooth
hands in welcome. He smiled at
us. His eyes were deep in a face
unlined, calm as the statue above
him.
Talking didn’t appear to be
called for, so I held out Huii
Doon’s chit. The yellow robe flut
tered in a draft of air, his hand
came out and he took the slip of
paper with Chinese characters.
"Huii Doon asks that I tell you
of the Emerald Buddha,” he said
musically. The voice was low,
clear, —like bells that tinkle
when you strike the rim of a wine
glass. His hands clapped, and a
servant came to lead the fright
ened horse away.
"Sit and rest,” he said. "You
shall refresh yourselves, then you
may listen.”
ANOTHER servant brought
wooden bowls of rice, and
we sat in a half circle before
that young priest and dipped into
the warm food. I was plenty
tired of rice, but hungry enough
not to object.
Li did not move. He looked
small beside his Buddha that al
most disappeared into the dark
roof, but he was a tall Chinese—
inches bigger than Bill Corkey,
who was over six feet.
"We’re listenin',” Bill sug
gested, wiping his mouth and set
ting down an empty bowl. "You
seem to know what we’re after,
so go ahead an’ try to make us
quit.”
That wasn’t any way to talk
to a priest, but Bill was right. We
couldn’t help it, but too many
people knew our business. I be
gan to wonder if the whole world
didn’t know more of it than we
did. The news had traveled from
Ternate to the edge of jungle
where the Tonle Sap Lake began,
to a Buddhist priest's temple. <
I sat between Bill and Hull I
Gim. The guide’s arm pressed
against me. It was trembling. I
looked at the bonze, and saw our
tiger rubbing himself against the
priest. I breathed deep, kept my
eyes on the animal, wonderin’ if
it was incense that made me see
things. No, there the beast stood,
startin' to purr as the bonze’s
hand scratched a big furry ear.
BILL closed his eyes. “It’s not
true, I don’t believe it,” he
said to himself. His eyes open,
and the striped cat’s still there.
"I never thought to see such a
thing—” Bill muttered, rubbing a
hand across his face.
The cat rubbed himself some
more, then sat down beside the
bonze, sleepy an’ contented. He
looked us over, green eyes blinkin’
in the candle light, an’ seemed to
decide we wouldn’t harm his mas
ter.
(To be continued)

DEADLOCKED
By International Jtewa Service
GENEVA, July 5. A state of
complete deadlock at the tri-partlte
naval arms limltrtion conference
here was reached today when the
naval experts’ committee adjourned
indefinitely ita labors “to permit the
various delegations to seek a means
of adjusting the cruiser needs of the
defferent powers.’
The deadlock resulted directly
from inability of the United States
to agree to Great Britain's demand
for 78 cruisers totaling about- 600,-
000 tons.
It was understood here today that
the United States delegation had
received instructions from Wash
ington that unless an agreement
on cruiser strength could be
reached, practically on the basis of
a proposal put forward by an
American delegate allowing 400,000
tons of cruiser strength each for
Great Britain and the United
States, that it would be better to
allow the cruiser question to rest
in abeyance until 1931.
The naval limitations treaties
signed at the Washington confer
ence, which have to do with capi
tal ships, expire in 1931 unless re
newed.
The American proposition repre
sented a compromise between the
United States's first proposal of
250,000 to 300,000 cruiser tonnage
each for Great Britain and the
United States, and the British de
mand for nearly 600,000 tons.
This American compromise propo
sition was presented at the experts’
committee meeting, and almost im
mediately the work of the commit
tee was adjourned without a set
date for another meeting.
The experts’ committee issued a
communique saying the adjourn
ment was taken with “a view to
permitting the various delegations
to seek a means of adjusting the
cruiser needs of the different
powers.”
The communique also declared an
agreement had been reached re
specting submarines, but later It
was authoritatively learned that
the agreement was only to limit
submarine torpedo tubes to 21
inches size, and that no agreement
had been reached on the tonnage
or number of submarines to be al
lowed the various powers.

Many merchants refused to com
ply with the regulation beca-ise they
were going away over the week-end
and had to put the flags out Satur

Others refused to ask employes
to come to work yesterday morning
just to hang out the flags As a
result in some cases ’he flags were
not out at all.
It is unlikely that the Commis
sioners will take any action on the
names before them. It is more likely
in view of the protest? that they /will
change the regulation.

ging the bed of the Hudson River

for the bodies of three unidentified

tragic holiday accident.

three young men went to their

When slavery was abolished in
the Brazils there sprang up to take
its place a traffic hardly less accursed,
the coolie trade. The coolie was a
Chinaman who agreed to leave his
country for a terui of years at stated
wages, but after a while vessels were
fitted out and dispatched to various
islands in the Pacific and other seas,
and thousands of natives were ab
ducted and sold into perpetual slav
ery. These abductod people also
came to be called coolies. Soon after
the trade opened the Chinese govern
ment, which received so much for
every coolie shipped away, began send
ing off its offenders and malefactors
along with honest laborers. Instead
of giving a thief the bastinado he was
sent to the coolie barracks; instead of
lopping off the head of a pirate he was
deported. Among every 500 coolies
were at least 100 malefactors, and re
volts at sea were of frequent occur
rence. In the year 1808 seventeen
vessels in the coolie trade were either
burned at sea or captured by the na
tives aboard, and in case of capture
not a man was spared.
Our ship, the Princess, of New
York, was lying at Macao, south of
Hong-Kong, in the fa'.l of 1868, when
four different craft were loading with
coolies at the same time. Macao was
one of the great shipping points, and
everything was carried on under the
supervision of government officials. I
saw a Brazilian brig take on 480
coolies, and a worse lot of men oould
not possibly have been scraped to
gether i'i any country. They were to
be housed between decks, and the brig
carried two cannon and ten soldiers to
keep them in subjection. They must
be looked after as sharply as convicts 1
on their way to Australia in the old j
days of the penal settlements. This j
brig, which was called the Don, got
away a week ahead of us, and,through
some blunder, with only a few of her
water-casks tilled. She had head
winds for five days, and we finally i
overtook her before she had made SOO j
miles on her long voyage. We sighted
her as the wind was dying out oue af
ternoon, and were carried down to '
within a mile of her before we lost
eteerageway. We could make out her
name with the glass, but even without
that the odor which came across the ;
water to on;'nostrils would have proved

served, alter shuttiug up his glass and

"That smell would settle it if I ;
was deaf, dumb ami blind and had no

It was about six o'clock, when we
lost the breeze, and the crew knocked

The brig was ull right, so far as we

"I'll tell you exactly what's taking

As I told you before, three of the

"Down with yon, lads, and prepare

As we had no firearms of any con

The sailors called out to our captain

Depopulation of Ireland.
In 1815 the decadence of Ireland
began in earnest, audit has kept tip :
ever since. In that year the popula- j
tion was 8,295,0)1, the high water ;
mark. On the 30th of last June it !
was 4,531,051, the lowest ever known. 1
While emigration has been robbing j
the nation of its vigorous sous and
daughters, the birth rate has been iu- ;
sufficient to fill up the gaps thus
made. The marriage rate has been j
only 5.8 per 1000 at best for the last j
ten years, while just now it is 0.3 j
under the miserable figure. The I
population decreased 13,084 between I
the first of April and the last of June.
It is estimated that in 17 years it will
be only 4,000,000. —Victor Smith in
New York Press.

bear unother up he will sink, and Instead

you will have two to drag out. When Christ
sprang out into the sea to deliver us, He
had no llie buoy. His Father did not help
Him. Alone in the wine press, alone In the

mountain,alone In the seal Oh, If Hesaves
us He shall havo all tho credit, for "there

battle charge in front of his troops, he had
a whole army to cheer him. AVhen Marshal
Ney sprang into the contest and plunged
In the spurs till the horse's flanks spurted

Behold, then, the spectacle of a drown

It was In 1948 when there were six English

He found a shoreman thut consented togo

If you have been much by the water, you

no help at all. Now, that Is just tho kind
of relief we want. Tho case Is urgent,

Bo quick, be quick! Oh, I wish you all

There was a man In the navy ut sea

The drowning man, brought to his senses,

The fluttering of tho bird kept him ou the

The world has had strojg swimmers be

the waters from Bluckwull pier to Graves

New modes have been inventod for res

"There Is none other name uuder heaven

The Missionaries and Temperance.
The fact of the advance of total absti
nence among the missionary body In India
received striking confirmation it a dinner
party on the Queen's birthday, given by
the collector of the district, when of the
nine missionary guests present all drank
to the health of her majesty In cold water.
The circumstance occasioned surprise, and
was the subject of much comment by the
kind host and Ills civilian friends. It is
rare to llnd a missionary of any nationality
In India to-day who takes liquor in any
form, except for medicinal purposes. Even
the German brethren, who indulge In their
beer at home, do not make a practice, we
aretold, of using It in India. The change of
sentiment In this matter during the last
j quarter of a century has been most
! marked. It is noteworthy that the Ameri
can missionaries had the honor of being
j tho pioneers in bringing about so desira
! ble a result. There was a time when they

"The Poor Man's Club."
The Rev. Walter B. Vassar writes as fol

Thesoclal side of the saloon Is the deadliest

Very strangely, we think, there are some

what Is called a "club"for the lowly. If a
tree may be known by its fruits, we may

He who condones Its presence in a com

Danger in the Nip.
| One of the surest ways of ending one's
days in a madhouse Is tostimulatea fagged
brain by constant "nips" of spirits. Over
| indulgence in spirits injures the memory to

Science Must Not Claim Too Much.
It is well that science has come to the aid
of temperauce. But science canuct win the
battle aloue. To say that alcohol acts so
and so upon the body makes no impressiou
upon the average mind. He needs the ap
peal to the heart. He needs the burning
words of women who have felt, and the im

needs the recital of the sufferings of

The country needs a new campaign of soul

everything save the need of immediate de
cision agalust the whisky devil—i'lio Up

INTO the limelight of our drama of
shifting evenis enters again the Chinese
coolie, bo warmly welcomed to
cur raclfic coast in the days of the
Argonauts, so despised a generation
later as to be forbidden our shores.
The Isthmian canal commission having
been assured by the high legal' authorities
of the government that the Chinese exclusion
and contract labor laws do not follow
the flag into the canal zone, and having
discovered that the Jamaica negroes upon
the big ditch are, at .<> cents an hour, taking
a week to finish a Yankee day's work,
is dickering with contractors for the de

livery of thousands of coolies at Panama.
This move has caused a cross-fire of pros
and cons from all parts of the land. Ignoring
the partisan questions Involved, >et us
consider the coolie from an Industrial,
economic and sociological point of view.
Coolies from the southern provinces of
China are asked for by the commission. In
other words, the celestials wanted for this
work are of the same type as the Chinese
laborers who were brought Into this country
until the Chinese exclusion laws were
passed. Practically our entire Chinese immigration,
while it lasted, consisted of Can- j
tonese ooolles, from the single province of
ICwan-tung, within forty miles of Canton,
Its capital. The fact Is that few Chinese
outside this region will ^nlgrate even to
countries where they are guaranteed full
civic rights. These Cantonese coolies were
collected In gangs by a Chinese, corporation
known as the Six Companies and shipped to
America under rlgtil contract to work for
a period of years fixed by their masters.
It was contended by agitators for Chinese
exclusion that this system was one of exploited
serfage. Slavery is. of course, the
order of the day in China. Children are purchased
from their parents, who sen them
on account of poverty. The slave girls
remain the property of their masters until
their marriage, when they become free. But
the males are slaves forever unless they
can purchase their freedom, and every chili
of a slave Is the property of the master.
Few, If any. of the coolies who have come
to America have belonged to ..ie actual
slave class. They are the very poor freemen,
forming the unskilled laborer class of
China.
*
* *
Th* coolie can underbid any white laborer
because his extreme poverty at home
has disciplined him to a life of abject privation
For many Interesting facts as to
how the coolie lives at home 1 am indebted
to Mr. Chester Holcombe, for many years
connected with our legation at Peking as
Interpreter, secretary of legation and acting
minister. Although meat is cheaper in
China than In our country, a laborer there
receiving what .ie considers good wages
cannot afford to eat a pound in a month.
Skilled laborers earn from 10 to HO cents in
silver per day, the average being about

coolies who. us they put It. "sell their

Imported abroad for such work as tnat in
Panama, earn at home from f> to 10 cents
for a long day's work, the average receiving
but 7 cents. And yet China is a country
where bachelors and old maids are unknown,
where each free man Has a large
family to support. To quote Mr. Holcombe:
"I have often hired a special messenger
t< trawl a distance of thirty miles for eight
cents, lioatmm are regularly hired to track
a native boat, pulling it against the stream,
fp :n Tientsin to Tungcho, a distance of i25
miles, for fifty cents and their food one
way. They make the return journey on
foot?that is, they travel a greater distance
than from Boston to New York for fifty
cents in silver ana one-nan wieir ioou.
The coolie's daily food Is steamed rice,
boiled cabbage ami a/few bits of raw turnip
pickled in strong brine. When very extravagant
lie purchases a handful of dried
watermelon seeds for out mill?our money?
and munches them for dessert. In summer
he var:es his diet with raw cucumbers, raw
carrots, raw turnips or perhaps a melon,
eaten rind and all. The very poor coolies
do not fare nearly so well as tills, the common
ration of the industrious workingman.
The coolie's marketing for his family dinner
and the fuel with whioli t?> cook it
costs him from three to four pieces of copper
"cash," each equal to about one mill,
our money. His charcoal costs him one
cash; rice or flour, two cash; cabbage, one
cash. On occasions of prosperity another
cash would be spent for oil or "soy," and
on very exceptional festive days still an_

spoonful of alcohol, to be drunk hot wiji

In summer the coolie's clothing' consists
of shoes, stockings, both of cotton;
unllned trousers, nnd sometimes a jacket,
all of cotton cloth. In spring and autumn.
if he can afford the luxury, his
Jacket an-i trousers are lined, and In tho
winter they are wadded, or, if he has
been very thrifty, he can perhaps keep
warmer in a jacket of sheepskin tanned
with th3 wool on It. I'nderclothlng is
unknown to him, and he sleeps in the
tutn.e garments In which he works. Thre-i
dollars more than suffices to buy his entire
summer wardrobe. His family of
five or six persons lives In a one-room
hut of brick laid In mud, with mud or
brick floor and paper windows.. This
altode has no chimney, the door offering
the only escape for smoke and foul air.
His entire furniture equipment consists of
a table a stool or two, sometimes a ward

robe and a platform of brick covered with

warms the bed.

The first large shipment of coolies to
America was made in 1855, when 8,526
were landed on the Pacific coast, under
contract. In spite of what might be
gathered from the exaggerated reports,
due to excitement over the exclusion question.
few of these celestial laborers were
ever brought to our own shores. When the
exclusion agitation was at its height there

Was ill or detained he always sent another

But the time soon came when contractors
no longer dared hire them for outdoor
labor. Then these 100,(100 Chinese already
in the country showed the American
that they could make a living for themselves

tic servants and have so far developed i

iers they are commanding higher wages In i

Chinatown. San Francisco, destroyed by

Now that the cry of cheap labor la no

rather than companions of the father
and husband, which latter, as soon as they
earn sufficient in this country, hurry home

This will not be the first employment of

There was a Chlnatow'n down In Panama

I am indebted for the following statement

i respectful class.'1

The housewifely woman can rejoice her

a horizontal border of red or blue,
h Instead, they are reversible, and woven In

softest and fleeciest wool Imaginable. White
' and pink, white and pale blue and whlta
e and pale green are the most popular comn
binatlons, the white forming the backs
ground on one side and the color on the
f other. Sometimes the blanket is woven in a
' checkerboard deMgn, and again in a huge
plaid, the checks of which are ten inches
e square. In white and r>ale blue, with the in

matching the color, these plaids are very effective.

edged with deep fringe, are a pretty novelty,
and arc likely to become popular, as
1 they are light of weight and are easily
a laundered. The old-time heavy Marseilles
? spread of massive pattern has given place

Ms. Editor As the editorial reporter for the
government paper has made pretty free use of my
name in connection with the second Hawaiian public
meeting, held Tuesday evening, October 19th, it is
but justice to myself to state one or two matters in
their true light. I am styled the " initiator of the
meeting, &c." So far from this being the case
First. I hod nothing whatever to do even with
the call for the first meeting. It was told me that
a meeting was to be held. I went, and did my duty
as a Hawaiian citizen.
Second. When it was proposed to hold a second
meeting, I expressed myself as decidedly opposed to
meeting again, knowing what uncertainty there
would be as to its success. However, the Hawoiians
went on and colled the meeting. I wish them to
succeed in such things, and or course attended as
before.
Third. The Resolution brought 'in by the Com
mittee, of which I was not Chairman was, the
most of it, thrust into the hands of the Committee as
they left Uip room, brought in hastily to furnish
il ' a- 1 1 - ,
eomeuiing to go to wont upon, ami oears evidence in
its very imperfection of its utterly unpremeditated
origin.
Some one has said , " that people is fit for freedom
that can hold a public meeting." It seems to be the
aim of some who call themselves friends of the Ha
waiian people to prove that they, the latter, and
themselves too perhaps, cannot hold a public meet
ing. It was such that were denounced in the meet
ing of last Tuesday night. C. J. Ltons.

tarifT resolutions, manifested great interest ; but
as soon as they were disposed of the audience be
gan to decrease, showing that these subjects mont
interested them.
The following are the resolutions as finally
adopted by the meeting : ' " -
1. Resolved, That the immigration into any coun--try
should be governed by the natural laws of supply
and demand, and by the advantages which that
country holds forth to settlers and laborers, and not
by governmental interference other than the bestowal
of such sums of money and iu such ways as may be
voted by the people. .
2. Resolved, That the further introduction into
this country of Chinese coolies is undesirable the
term "coolie being understood to mean Asiatm
male laborers bound to service for a term of years.
3. Resolved, That as tbe Ihws of this kingdom do
especially favor the introduction of such laborers, by
legalizing foreign contracts for service, and by pro
viding a penal enactment for the enforcement of
such contracts, it is advisable that the law to that
effect be repealed.
4. Resolved, That laws, enforcing contracts to
service by penal enactment, tend to injustice, and
are' contrary to the spirit of the age - ..-
5. Resolved, That we do approve of the appro
priation of governmental funds to tbe assistance of
Pacific Islanders who come here free of contract, as
permanent settlers.
6. Resolved, That in consideration of tbe high
tariffs of our commercial neighbor the United States,
it is expedient for ns to devise some means for pro
tecting our own industrial interests in like manner.
7. That an executive committee of five be appoint
ed by the chair, to prepare a petition to the next
Legislative Assembly, in the Hawaiian and English
languages, embodying these resolutions, and urging
upon that body the consideration of the principles
therein enunciated ; the said committee to cause the
same to be circulated for signatures throughout the
Islands.
In accordance with the last resolution a Com
mittee was appointed to draw up and circulate a
petition to the Legislature, embodying tho senti
ments adopted by the meeting.
After the passage of tho resolutions, a commu
nication, addressed to the meeting, was read from
twenty or more of the leading Chinese merchants,
expressing sympathy with the citizens in the pur
pose of their meeting, and earnestly protesting
against the further introduction of their country
men as coolies. The letter waa ordered to be pub
lished, and will appear next week.
Tbe absence from this meeting of the few sym
pathizers with the ministry, who had attended
tho two previous meetings, with the evident de
sign of disturbing, waa a noteworthy circum
stance, as showing preconcerted action in their
movements.
The meeting adjourned sine die.

OUR SEVENTH VOLUME.
Rt aders, with the present bsue coni*
merues the Seventh Volume of the
Workingman's Advocatr For six
v^airs, in sunshine and storm, through
eiil and through good report, through
prosperity and adversity, it has aimed
to battle for the right and plead the
cause of the oppressed, and it is cer
tainly a source of gratification to he
enabled, at ibis date, to look back upon
its course and tru'hfully claim that it
lias steadfastly endeavored, under all
circumstances, to stand by its colors,
and the principles it w as established to
repiescnt and defend. More we do
not desire and claim ; this much, we
beheve its friends.and even its enemies
(if any should be found) will cheerfully
concede.
>or does it claim especial credit for
such a record. To Lave proved re
creant to the interests of humanity and
labor, would have deservedly stamped
it with infamy; hnt it does claim that
such a record is the best recommenda
tion which can be used to commend it
to the support of the working classes
throughout the United State*.
To its thousands of readers, scatter
ed throughout the States and Tcnito
ries, it returns the thanks of a grateful
heart; to the fr. r.ds of reform the world
over, it opens la columns; to the labor
organizations, whose interests it repre
sents, it returns its especial acknowl
edgements ; to the principles of the
National Labor Union it acknowlt dges
renewed allegiance; :o the oppressor*
of mankind it promises undying hostil
ity, and to the public in general, it re
iterates its annual pledge—that so long
as it is published it will know no isms,
do issues, no religion, do politics, but
the isms, the issues, the religion and
the politics of Labor.
A. C. Cameron.
A REVIEW.
The National Labor Congress will
hold its fifth annual session at Cincin
nati, commencing on Monday, the 15th
of August, From the first session at
Baltimore to the present time, there
has been a steady development of the
purposes of the industrial classes, at
tested in numberless ways, and in none
more conspiciously than by greater in
dependent political action.
Special remedial effoits against speci
fic wrongs, a demand for a more ra
tional division of fitne, fewer hours for
exhaustive labor and more for instruc
tion and recreation ; an indefinite ex
pectaiion of relief from co-operative
societies, and a vague anticipation that
the existing political parties might, if1
not from considerations of justice, at
least from a desire to acquire votes, as
sume the cause of labor and attempt to
remove its legal oppressions, were the
utmost hopes of the Baltimore Con
gress. It were need I ms to recount
here the circumstances which dispu
ted all such hopes. It soon became
evident enough to the working classes
that the hesitant speech of their first
Congress was mist .ken, by the law
makers and politicians, as the indefinite
longings and the erode ambitions of
mechanics and working men, who had
not sufficient intelligence to shape their
demands into a political platform ; nor
sufficient manhood to defend them by
""independent political action.
I n€ lucceeauig jobt uiic ui piu
greM to tbe workingmen, and when
their representatives inet at Chicago
their heeitant apeech and vague pur
pope were replaced by a definite body
of principles and fixed determined reso
lotidns. The Chicago Congress was
no longer satisfied w ith eight hours a
day, or deluded byexpectations of sup
port from the politicians. It w?fit
to the root of the antagi nisro between
__«apital and labor; declared this antag
onism to result from fiscal and legal j
contrivances, and demanded that the
legislative power, as it had created
should remove it. It declared that
espital ie the product, (he child of
labor, not it* creator aud parent; and
that it is tbe reversal of this natural
relation which is the source of the evils
of which the industrial classes complain.
When the legislative powers first en
dowed capital, by ursury snd other laws,
with a quality of growth disproportion
«Pto the power of growth inherent in
the firal legislative establishment
of human slavery was elected, the first
abridgement of the equality of human
rights was accomplished, and th? foun
dations were laid of that fraud which
has ennobled and enriched ihe tew, de
based and impoverished the many, and
v which is ss potent to-day, in iu oppres
jgm powers, under our form# of politi
MMhfreedosa as under despotisms, un
jushied by a single recognition af the
equality of human rights. The Chica
go Congress declared this legislative
basis of antagonism should be abolish
ed, and boldly aiiaouuccd a platform of
l>rincipl«s which would effect tins and,
tnd resolved to maintain it by political
tot ion.
11ns plat term and this resolve have I
been re asserted in every subsequent i
Congress, and w hatever hesitancy or
doubt which may heretofore have exist 1
ed, among working men, as to the pol i
icy of forming a distinct political party, «
there certainly remains none at present.
With the experience of the last few years
to expect relief, or hope for aid to labor,
as against monopoly, from existing
] parties as now organized, is driveling
| idiocy. No protest lias been able to
arrest the plunder of the public domain,
and the Congress just adjourned has

THE EFFECTS OF WAR
The almost immediate effect which
any considerable warlike commotion in
any considerable country has upon the
1 industrial interests of the rest of the
world, should invite the attention of all
thinking workingmen. It is palpable
that while some sections only are seri
ously injured by foreign wars immedi
ately after they commence,others which
are apparently henelitted, are always
sure to fall in ultimately for a share of
the loss. Especially is this true of coun
tries which depend principally upon
their manufacturing industries. A w ar
between two great powers, such as the
two which are now grappling and snarl
ing at each other, (thoughwhat benefit
is going to occur to either at a people >
let the result he w hat it may, we cannot
conci ive,) takes a vast lumber from the
various industries for soldiers, and nat
urally creates an increased demand for
the products of foreign lands. This
increased or inflated demand for the
products of a people is generally con
sidered to be a benefit to them, and a
something to rejoice at, but it creates j
an abnormal market, and the reaction
in trade which is inevitable after a de
claration of peace, sends many to
bankruptcy and penury. Our own
war in its result, as apparent to-day,
upon our people as well as upon those j
of the rest of the world, is good ami
sufficient evidence of the correctness of
this proposition. During its continu
ance the great body of the producers
who had entered the army, left employ
ment enough at home for all the labor
ers left at home. Manufacturers and
merchants were incited by the spirit of
speculation which then attained such a
wide spread and deplored popularity j
among those haBly to become rich, at i
whatever cost to others,to run up prices.
This move encouraged and made it
fieceifcary for laboring men to demand
more wages, and they in number being
below the demand w ere in a position to
enforce their demands. Bet the war,
as must everything else, terminated.
The ar^ty returned home, and aonght
the fbrm ud the fentory. The menu
faeluring centres were overrun with
workers clamoring for employment.
The capitalist still insisting upon wsr
profits,was unwilling to pay war wages,
and the supply >1 laborers being sc
greatly in area* of the demaud, they
were in a position to enforce their sel
ti*h objects. The same effect will ac
crue from like cause to any people,
whether the war be upon home or upon
foreign soil, so that it is of sufficient
magnitude. Of course the campaign
of a week or a mouth will be ot no
great detriment to any outside of those
immediately affected, but man foments
wars, but God awards the victory, and
tempers their duration w'ith His wis
! dom, for he is a just God, and has de
creed that the victory shall not be al

are a formidable copartner slop War

; To the Kditor of the H obbin«mabV Advocatb :
I suppose that by this time the news
has reached C'hicago of the action, or
rather uon-action of the New Y'nrk Cen
tral Labor Union. It has virtually re
fused to appoint delegates to represent
it at the Cincinnati Congress appointed
for the 15th inst. I fear there will l>e
but a slim delegation from this State,
although I trust the German Unions1
will be well represented. My opinion,
expressed in your columns more than a
year ago,however, remains unchanged.
The Labor Reform must be accom
plished, and will Ire by the actinr. of the '
workingmen, and I hope, working wo-1
men, of the West and South. The!
j Cougress, since its institution,has done '
| its work well; it has never once met [
without accomplishing a great result.
From Baltimore we received an address
worthy to be placed with the great
' State papers of the Revolution. At1
I Chicago was laid the foundation stone
of the Labor Temple, the freedom of
' the land. New York opened the doors
t of the Union to the millions of women

cultural and mechanical labor in the'
admission of the soil tillers of the South.
I feel assured that the forthcoming
Cincinnati Congress wid also leave its
mark, and I trust that the hardy pio

as the eastern churches are beginning

tip. I fear cheap labor will triumph.
They will make good enough Boston
Christian.., that is “believers in Christ,
with Christ left out.'1

ick Douglas and other colored men, bnt

ganizations. It seems to me to be a

The coal miner* of this region hare

and are still out. The cause of the
difficulty was that we had been receiv
ing one dollar per too, which the em

tional Association. We believe it is
the dnty of every miner and working
man in tbis section to join our organi
zation, and we believe they will.

How Chinamen Get to America.
It may be interesting to tbnse who are
not posted to know bow John Chinamen got
here. It is to be understood, first of all
that the Chinese who come to America are
poor men. An indigent Chinamen goes to
the official of his native village or the pre
cinct of the city in which he resides and
1 propose* to mortgage himself and family for
money with which to come to America,
1 where be expects to make his fortune, clear
up bis affairs, and live thenceforward in
peace, comfort and prosperity. It often
i happens, however, that the Chinaman does
not return at the expiration of a certain
time for which the mortgage runs, and then
the harpies and money-lenders, into whose
hands the business has fallen by this time,
pounce upon the poor Chinaman's family
and sell them into slavery without the least
compunction of conscience

Tbe G. M. submitted bis report setting

ex'-ess of any previous year ; an increase of

istence at the last session, while it also set

vania. and No. 7 in New York. A num

whole tbe organization was shown to be in

The report was accepted and referred to
the appropriate committee.

Pa.; also, from Indiana and West Virginia,

otherwise.

nual convention
None of the regular 'orumittees being pre

M'Cormick in the chair.
The Committee on Grievances submitted
a report relative to difficulties in existence
among the fraternity in Philadelphia and
bfj.iriin, Ohio. A plan of adjustment was
recommended, which on motion was adopted.
The committee to whom was referred the
G. M.’* (Edwards) report submitted a re
port highly eulogizing his services during
the last year and the increased prosperity of
the organization during his administration,
it showing an increase of almost one hundred
per cent, during the last two years. They
concluded their report by offering a resolu
tion discontinuing the usual tax upon initia
tions ; also the form of a new traveling card
for members.
The report was accepted, ana the resolu
tions were adopted as presented.
Mr. M'Kema. of Bethlehem, Pa , gub
mitted a paper containing rules and regula

their funeral expense* and the supj>ort of
their widows and orphans. The subject
gave rise to quite a lengthy and animated
discussion, pending which the hour of ad
journment arrived, when the body adjourn
e<l to meet Friday morning at 9.30.
